content.explanation: An access port carries traffic for exactly one VLAN, untagged. A trunk port carries traffic for multiple VLANs over a single physical link — to keep each frame's VLAN membership straight as it crosses the link, the switch inserts an 802.1Q tag into the frame identifying which VLAN it belongs to. The one VLAN a trunk doesn't tag (by default) is called the native VLAN.
content.example:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,99
 switchport trunk native vlan 99
# VLANs 10 and 20 cross this link tagged; VLAN 99 crosses untagged (native)

check.statement: Fill in the blank: a trunk port uses 802.1Q ____ to identify which VLAN each frame belongs to as it crosses the link.

solution.explanation: 802.1Q tagging inserts a small header into each frame carrying its VLAN ID, so the switch on the other end of the trunk knows which VLAN to deliver it into — without tags, a shared trunk link couldn't distinguish traffic from different VLANs.
